content.content:
1→---
     2→name: nw-tdd-methodology
     3→description: Deep knowledge for Outside-In TDD - double-loop architecture, ATDD integration, port-to-port testing, walking skeletons, and test doubles policy
     4→user-invocable: false
     5→disable-model-invocation: true
     6→---
     7→
     8→# Outside-In TDD Methodology
     9→
    10→## Double-Loop TDD Architecture
    11→
    12→Outer loop: ATDD/E2E Tests (customer view) - business requirements, hours-days to green.
    13→Inner loop: Unit Tests (developer view) - technical implementation, minutes to green, RED->GREEN->REFACTOR.
    14→
    15→Outer stays red while inner cycles. Outer drives WHAT to build, inner drives HOW.
    16→Never build components not needed by actual user scenarios.
    17→
    18→## Outside-In vs Inside-Out
    19→
    20→Inside-Out (Classic/bottom-up): discovers collaborators through refactoring. TDD guides design completely.
    21→Outside-In (London/top-down/mockist): knows collaborators upfront, mocks them, implements each moving inward.
    22→
    23→Use Outside-In when: architectural boundaries known (hexagonal), program to interface not implementation.
    24→
    25→## ATDD Integration (Lightweight)
    26→
    27→Original 2008 heavyweight ATDD was "too heavyweight for most real teams." Updated approach (Hendrickson 2024):
    28→- Few Given/When/Then examples, not many | Separate requirements from tests
    29→- Smallest subset of team with relevant skills | Value = shared understanding, not executable specs
    30→- Automate only where high-value
    31→
    32→## BDD Integration
    33→
    34→BDD emerged from Outside-In TDD. Given(context)->When(action)->Then(outcome) maps to outside-in mindset.
    35→BDD reframes TDD as design/specification technique, not just testing. More accessible to stakeholders.
    36→Gherkin: structured format bridging technical/non-technical. Use pragmatically - automate only where high value.
    37→
    38→## Outside-In Development Workflow (Bache)
    39→1. Write Guiding Test (acceptance) from user perspective - thick slice of functionality
    40→2. Start at top-level entry point, design collaborating classes incrementally
    41→3. Use mocks to experiment with interfaces/protocols
    42→4. As each layer implemented, move to previously mocked collaborators, TDD again
    43→5. Never build what isn't needed for actual user scenarios
    44→
    45→## Port-to-Port Testing
    46→
    47→Tests enter through driving port (application service / public API) and assert outcomes at driven port boundaries.
    48→Internal classes (entities, value objects, domain services) exercised indirectly - never instantiated directly in test code.
    49→
    50→Flow: Driving Port -> Application -> Domain -> Driven Port (mocked)
    51→
    52→```python
    53→def test_order_service_processes_payment():
    54→    # Setup - mock driven port (external dependency)
    55→    payment_gateway = MockPaymentGateway()
    56→    order_repo = InMemoryOrderRepository()
    57→
    58→    # Test through driving port (application service)
    59→    order_service = OrderService(payment_gateway, order_repo)
    60→    result = order_service.place_order(customer_id, items)
    61→
    62→    # Assert observable outcomes
    63→    assert result.is_confirmed()
    64→    payment_gateway.verify_charge_called(amount=100.00)
    65→```
    66→
    67→## Unit of Behavior (not Unit of Code)
    68→
    69→Test = story about the problem your code solves. Granularity related to stakeholder needs.
    70→A unit of behavior may span multiple classes. Test from driving port to driven port boundary.
    71→Key question: "Can you explain this test to a stakeholder?" If not, you're testing implementation details.
    72→
    73→## Classical vs Mockist Verification
    74→
    75→Classical TDD: real objects | state verification | less coupled to implementation | survives refactoring better.
    76→Mockist TDD: mocks for objects with behavior | behavior verification | lighter setup | more coupled to impl.
    77→Best practice: combine strategically. Behavior verification at layer boundaries, state verification within layers.
    78→
    79→## Test Doubles Taxonomy (Meszaros)
    80→- Dummy: passed but never used
    81→- Fake: working impl with shortcuts (in-memory DB)
    82→- Stub: predefined answers
    83→- Spy: stub that records interactions
    84→- Mock: pre-programmed with expectations for behavior verification
    85→
    86→Choose type by need: mock for interaction design | stub when don't care about interaction | fake for integration bridge.
    87→
    88→## Hexagonal Architecture Testing Strategy
    89→
    90→### Domain Layer
    91→Tested indirectly through driving port (application service) unit tests with real domain objects.
    92→Domain entities, value objects, domain services are implementation details. Testing them directly couples tests to internal structure.
    93→
    94→Exception: Standalone domain logic with complex algorithms (e.g., pricing engine) MAY be tested directly when complexity warrants it and the class has a stable public interface. This is the EXCEPTION, not the rule.
    95→
    96→### Application Layer
    97→Classical TDD within layer, Mockist TDD at port boundaries.
    98→Use real Order, Money, Customer objects in application service tests.
    99→Mock IPaymentGateway, IEmailService ports when testing orchestration.
   100→
   101→### Infrastructure Layer
   102→Integration tests ONLY - no unit tests for adapters.
   103→Mocking infrastructure inside an adapter test is testing the mock, not the adapter.
   104→Use real infrastructure (testcontainers, in-memory databases) to verify actual behavior.
   105→
   106→### E2E Tests
   107→Minimal mocking - only truly external systems (3rd party APIs beyond your control).
   108→Use real domain services, application services, repositories.
   109→
   110→## Test Doubles Policy
   111→
   112→Acceptable (port boundaries only):
   113→- `Mock<IPaymentGateway>` - external payment service port
   114→- `Mock<IEmailService>` - external email provider port
   115→- `InMemoryUserRepository` - fake for fast tests (implements IUserRepository port)
   116→
   117→Do not mock inside the hexagon:
   118→- Domain entities (Order, Customer) - use real objects
   119→- Value objects (Money, Email) - cheap to create, deterministic
   120→- Application services (OrderProcessor) - use real with mocked ports
   121→- Domain services (PricingService) - use real objects
   122→
   123→## Walking Skeleton Protocol
   124→
   125→At most one walking skeleton per new feature. When `is_walking_skeleton: true` in roadmap:
   126→- Write exactly ONE E2E/acceptance test proving end-to-end wiring
   127→- Implement thinnest possible slice - hardcoded values, minimal branching
   128→- Do NOT write unit tests - the E2E test IS the deliverable
   129→- Do NOT add error handling, edge cases, or validation
   130→- Skip inner TDD loop, go directly from RED_ACCEPTANCE to GREEN
   131→
   132→E2E tests are inherently slow and flaky. The walking skeleton is the ONE justified E2E test per feature.
   133→
   134→## E2E Test Management
   135→
   136→Enable ONE E2E test at a time to prevent commit blocks:
   137→1. All E2E tests except first one marked with skip/ignore
   138→2. Complete first scenario through Outside-In TDD
   139→3. Commit working implementation
   140→4. Enable next E2E test
   141→5. Repeat until all scenarios implemented
   142→
   143→## Step Method Pattern
   144→
   145→Step methods call production services, not test infrastructure:
   146→
   147→```csharp
   148→[When("business action occurs")]
   149→public async Task WhenBusinessActionOccurs()
   150→{
   151→    var service = _serviceProvider.GetRequiredService<IBusinessService>();
   152→    _result = await service.PerformBusinessActionAsync(_testData);
   153→}
   154→```
   155→
   156→Scaffold unimplemented collaborators with `NotImplementedException`:
   157→```csharp
   158→throw new NotImplementedException(
   159→    "Business capability not yet implemented - driven by outside-in TDD"
   160→);
   161→```
   162→
   163→## Business-Focused Testing
   164→
   165→### Unit Test Naming
   166→- Class pattern: `<DrivingPort>Should`
   167→- Method pattern: `<ExpectedOutcome>_When<SpecificBehavior>[_Given<Preconditions>]`
   168→- Example: `AccountServiceShould.IncreaseBalance_WhenDepositMade_GivenSufficientFunds`
   169→
   170→### Behavior Types
   171→- Command behavior: changes system state (Given-When-Then)
   172→- Query behavior: returns state projection (Given-Then)
   173→- Process behavior: orchestrates multiple commands/queries
   174→
   175→### Test Structure
   176→- Arrange: set up business context and test data
   177→- Act: perform business action
   178→- Assert: validate business outcome and state changes
   179→
   180→## Environment-Adaptive Testing
   181→- Local development: in-memory infrastructure for fast feedback (~100ms)
   182→- CI/CD pipeline: production-like infrastructure for integration validation (~2-5s)
   183→- Same scenarios: single source of truth across all environments
   184→

1→---
     2→name: nw-quality-framework
     3→description: Quality gates - 11 commit readiness gates, build/test protocol, validation checkpoints, and quality metrics
     4→user-invocable: false
     5→disable-model-invocation: true
     6→---
     7→
     8→# Quality Framework
     9→
    10→## Commit Readiness Gates (11)
    11→
    12→All pass before committing:
    13→
    14→1. Active acceptance test passes (not skipped, not ignored)
    15→2. All unit tests pass
    16→3. All integration tests pass
    17→4. All other enabled tests pass
    18→5. Code formatting validation passes
    19→6. Static analysis passes
    20→7. Build validation passes (all projects)
    21→8. No test skips in execution (ignores OK during progressive implementation)
    22→9. Test count within behavior budget
    23→10. No mocks inside hexagon
    24→11. Business language in tests verified
    25→
    26→Note: Reviewer approval (formerly Gate 12) and Testing Theater detection (formerly Gate 13) enforced at deliver-level Phase 4 (Adversarial Review via /nw-review), not per step.
    27→
    28→## Quality Gates by Category
    29→- **Architecture**: all layers touched | integration points validated | stack proven E2E | pipeline functional
    30→- **Implementation**: real functionality (not placeholders) | automated pipeline | happy path coverage | production patterns
    31→- **Business Value**: meaningful user value | testable AC | measurable success metrics
    32→- **Real Data**: golden masters present | edge cases tested | no silent errors | API assumptions documented
    33→- **Test Integrity**: every test falsifiable | behavioral assertions only | no circular verification | no mock-dominated tests | no assertion-free tests (see Testing Theater Self-Check in agent core)
    34→
    35→## Build and Test Protocol
    36→
    37→After every TDD cycle, Mikado leaf, or atomic transformation:
    38→
    39→```bash
    40→# 1. BUILD
    41→dotnet build --configuration Release --no-restore
    42→
    43→# 2. TEST
    44→dotnet test --configuration Release --no-build --verbosity minimal
    45→
    46→# 2.5. QUALITY VALIDATION (before committing)
    47→# - Edge cases tested (null, empty, malformed, boundary)
    48→# - No silent error handling (all errors logged/alerted)
    49→# - Real data golden masters included where applicable
    50→# - API assumptions documented
    51→
    52→# 3. COMMIT (if tests pass)
    53→# Use appropriate format below
    54→
    55→# 4. ROLLBACK (if tests fail)
    56→git reset --hard HEAD^  # Maintain 100% green discipline
    57→```
    58→
    59→For commit message formats, load the collaboration-and-handoffs skill.
    60→
    61→## Validation Checkpoints
    62→- **Pre-work**: all tests passing | code smell detection complete | execution plan created
    63→- **During work**: atomic transformation safety | 100% test pass rate | commit after each step | level sequence adherence
    64→- **Post-work**: quality metrics quantified | architectural compliance validated | test suite integrity maintained
    65→
    66→## Quality Metrics
    67→
    68→Track: cyclomatic complexity (reduction) | maintainability index (improvement) | technical debt ratio (reduction) | test coverage (maintenance) | test effectiveness (75-80% mutation kill rate at Phase 2.25) | code smells (systematic elimination across 22 types).
    69→
    70→For mutation testing integration, load the property-based-testing skill.
    71→
    72→## Object Calisthenics (Application + Domain Layers)
    73→
    74→9 design constraints for clean OOP code in the hexagonal core (Jeff Bay,
    75→ThoughtWorks Anthology). Apply during GREEN and COMMIT phases.
    76→
    77→### Rules
    78→
    79→| # | Rule | Rationale | Layer |
    80→|---|------|-----------|-------|
    81→| 1 | One indentation level per method | Forces decomposition | Domain, Application |
    82→| 2 | No `else` keyword | Guard clauses, early returns | Domain, Application |
    83→| 3 | Wrap all primitives and strings | Value objects | Domain |
    84→| 4 | First-class collections | Domain collection types | Domain |
    85→| 5 | One dot per line | Law of Demeter | Domain, Application |
    86→| 6 | No abbreviations | Intention-revealing names | All |
    87→| 7 | Small entities (<50 LOC classes, <10 LOC methods) | SRP | Domain, Application |
    88→| 8 | Max 2 instance variables per class | Promotes decomposition | Domain |
    89→| 9 | No getters/setters | Tell, don't ask | Domain, Application |
    90→
    91→### Rule 9 Relaxation Policy
    92→
    93→Getters are acceptable in these cases:
    94→- DTOs/response objects at port boundaries (serialization needs)
    95→- CQRS read models (query-optimized projections)
    96→- Value objects with computed properties (e.g., Money.amount)
    97→- Framework requirements (ORM mapping, serialization)
    98→
    99→Rule 9 applies strictly to domain entities and application services.
   100→Behavior through commands, not data access.
   101→
   102→### Scope
   103→
   104→- Applies to: Domain layer, Application layer (inside the hexagon)
   105→- Does NOT apply to: Adapters, infrastructure, DTOs, configuration
   106→- Enforcement phase: GREEN (writing new code) + COMMIT (refactoring)
   107→

1→---
     2→name: nw-fp-principles
     3→agent: nw-functional-software-crafter
     4→description: Core functional programming thinking patterns and type system foundations, language-agnostic
     5→user-invocable: false
     6→disable-model-invocation: true
     7→---
     8→
     9→# FP Principles
    10→
    11→Core functional programming thinking patterns. Language-agnostic.
    12→
    13→Cross-references: [fp-domain-modeling](./fp-domain-modeling.md) | [fp-hexagonal-architecture](./fp-hexagonal-architecture.md) | [fp-algebra-driven-design](./fp-algebra-driven-design.md)
    14→
    15→---
    16→
    17→## 1. Higher-Order Functions as Problem Decomposition
    18→
    19→[STARTER]
    20→
    21→Three operations replace most loops:
    22→
    23→| Operation | Purpose | Replaces |
    24→|-----------|---------|----------|
    25→| **Map** | Transform each element, preserve structure | Loop building new collection |
    26→| **Filter** | Keep elements matching condition | Loop with conditional |
    27→| **Fold** | Accumulate elements into single result | Loop with running total |
    28→
    29→**When to use Map**: Transform every element without changing collection shape. Nested maps handle nested structures.
    30→**When to use Filter**: Select elements without changing their values.
    31→**When to use Fold**: Reduce collection to single value. Accumulator IS your state. Combining function IS your state transition. Folds make state machines explicit.
    32→
    33→**Decision**: "Am I transforming, selecting, or accumulating?" Pick matching operation. If none fit, compose two.
    34→
    35→**Why**: These operations communicate intent. Map says "same shape, different values." Fold says "many inputs, one output." Loops say nothing about intent until you read every line.
    36→
    37→---
    38→
    39→## 2. Type-Driven Design
    40→
    41→[STARTER]
    42→
    43→Write the type signature before implementation. The type tells you what the function can and cannot do.
    44→
    45→**Process**:
    46→1. Declare what the function consumes and produces
    47→2. Ask: "which type-specific operations do I actually use?"
    48→3. Replace concrete types with type variables for everything you don't inspect
    49→4. Add constraints only for capabilities you use (equality, ordering, display)
    50→
    51→**Design progression**: Concrete types -> type variables -> constrained type variables. Each step increases reuse while documenting minimal assumptions.
    52→
    53→**Why**: Function's type signature is a contract. Narrower types mean fewer possible implementations, fewer bugs.
    54→
    55→---
    56→
    57→## 3. Pattern Matching as Decision Decomposition
    58→
    59→[STARTER]
    60→
    61→Decompose decisions by data shape, not boolean conditions. Each clause handles one concrete case. Compiler verifies exhaustiveness.
    62→
    63→**When to use pattern matching**: "What shape is this data?"
    64→**When to use guards/conditions**: "What property does this value have?"
    65→**When to use named bindings**: Intermediate results need a name to avoid repetition.
    66→
    67→**Heuristic**: Prefer small extracted functions over giant match expressions. Pattern match on top-level shape, delegate to named functions for sub-decisions.
    68→
    69→**Exhaustiveness as safety net**: When you add a new variant to a choice type, compiler flags every match that doesn't handle it.
    70→
    71→---
    72→
    73→## 4. Composition Patterns
    74→
    75→[INTERMEDIATE]
    76→
    77→### Partial Application
    78→
    79→Fix some arguments of a general function to create specialized version. Eliminates throwaway helper functions.
    80→
    81→**When**: General function exists and you need specialized version for specific context.
    82→
    83→### Function Composition (Pipelines)
    84→
    85→Chain functions where output of one feeds into next. Each function has single responsibility.
    86→
    87→**Why**: Composition reveals architecture of computation. Pipelines read as sequence of steps, making business process visible.
    88→
    89→### Point-Free Style
    90→
    91→Omit explicit argument when function is just a composition. Use when it reveals intent. Avoid when it obscures meaning.
    92→
    93→---
    94→
    95→## 5. Container Abstractions
    96→
    97→[INTERMEDIATE] -> [ADVANCED]
    98→
    99→Progressive hierarchy for working with values inside containers (nullables, lists, futures, results).
   100→
   101→### [INTERMEDIATE] Transformable Container (Functor)
   102→
   103→**What**: Apply function to values inside container without changing structure.
   104→**Plain English**: "I have a value in a box. Transform the value without opening the box."
   105→**When**: You have nullable/optional/list/future and want to transform contents without inspecting the container.
   106→**Guarantees**: Transforming with identity does nothing. Can fuse or split transformations freely.
   107→
   108→### [INTERMEDIATE] Combinable Containers (Applicative)
   109→
   110→**What**: Apply a function inside a container to values inside other containers.
   111→**Plain English**: "I have a function in a box AND values in boxes. Combine them."
   112→**When**: Validation -- check multiple fields independently, combine results only if all succeed. Doesn't short-circuit; collects all errors.
   113→
   114→### [INTERMEDIATE] Combinable Values (Monoid)
   115→
   116→**What**: Combine two values of same type into one, with default element that changes nothing.
   117→**Plain English**: "I have many values. Smash them together into one."
   118→**When**: Folding/reducing collections. Combining operation must be associative, enabling parallelism.
   119→**Examples**: String concatenation with empty string | addition with zero | list append with empty list.
   120→
   121→### [ADVANCED] Chainable Operations (Monad)
   122→
   123→**What**: Chain operations where each step produces wrapped value, next step depends on previous result.
   124→**Plain English**: "Step 1's output determines what step 2 does. Each step might fail/branch/have effects."
   125→**When**: Sequential dependent operations where each step can fail, branch, or produce effects.
   126→
   127→### Decision Tree: Which Abstraction Do I Need?
   128→
   129→```
   130→Do I need to transform values inside a container?
   131→  YES, one function, one container --> Transformable (Functor)
   132→  YES, combine multiple independent containers --> Combinable Containers (Applicative)
   133→  YES, chain dependent operations sequentially --> Chainable Operations (Monad)
   134→Do I need to combine values of the same type?
   135→  YES --> Combinable Values (Monoid)
   136→```
   137→
   138→### Progression Summary
   139→
   140→Each level adds a new kind of combination:
   141→- **Transformable**: one function, one container
   142→- **Combinable Containers**: one function, multiple containers (independent)
   143→- **Chainable**: sequential dependent operations, each producing container
   144→- **Combinable Values**: same-type values collapsed into one
   145→
   146→### Runnable Example: Map, Filter, Fold on Domain Objects
   147→
   148→```
   149→orders = [Order(100, "pending"), Order(250, "shipped"), Order(50, "pending")]
   150→
   151→pendingTotals = orders
   152→  |> filter (o -> o.status == "pending")    -- [Order(100, "pending"), Order(50, "pending")]
   153→  |> map (o -> o.amount)                    -- [100, 50]
   154→  |> fold 0 (acc, x -> acc + x)            -- 150
   155→```
   156→
   157→---
   158→
   159→## 6. Specialized Chainable Patterns
   160→
   161→[ADVANCED]
   162→
   163→| Pattern | What It Manages | When to Use |
   164→|---------|----------------|-------------|
   165→| **Optional** (Maybe/Option) | Possible absence | Operations that can fail without explanation |
   166→| **Result** (Either) | Failure with context | Operations that fail with error details |
   167→| **Environment** (Reader) | Shared read-only config | Dependency injection, configuration threading |
   168→| **Accumulator** (Writer) | Side-channel output | Logging, auditing, collecting metadata |
   169→| **Stateful** (State) | Sequential state changes | Counters, parsers, accumulators |
   170→
   171→These compose: real applications stack multiple patterns. See [fp-hexagonal-architecture](./fp-hexagonal-architecture.md) for DI patterns.
   172→
   173→---
   174→
   175→## 7. Lazy Evaluation as Design Pattern
   176→
   177→[INTERMEDIATE]
   178→
   179→Separate WHAT to compute from WHEN it gets computed. Define potentially infinite sequences and let consumer determine how much to evaluate.
   180→
   181→**When**: Generating candidates then selecting results | pagination and streaming | decoupling producers from consumers | build systems that only rebuild what changed.
   182→
   183→**Separation principle**: Generate all possibilities, then filter. Declarative style says WHAT you want, not HOW to search.
   184→
   185→---
   186→
   187→## 8. The FP Problem-Solving Method
   188→
   189→[STARTER]
   190→
   191→1. **Start with type signature**: What does this function consume and produce?
   192→2. **Identify traversal pattern**: Map, filter, fold, or search?
   193→3. **Recognize accumulator**: If folding, what is the state and how does each element change it?
   194→4. **Decompose by data shape**: Pattern match on constructors, handle each case independently
   195→5. **Compose small functions**: Build complex behavior from simple, tested pieces
   196→
   197→**Mindset shift**: Describe WHAT to compute (transformations, compositions, constraints) rather than HOW (loops, mutations, control flow).
   198→
   199→| Imperative Thinking | Functional Thinking |
   200→|---------------------|---------------------|
   201→| Loop through items | Map/filter/fold over collections |
   202→| Mutate variables | Transform immutable values |
   203→| Check conditions with if/else | Pattern match on data shapes |
   204→| Inherit from base class | Satisfy capability constraints |
   205→| Call methods on objects | Compose functions into pipelines |
   206→| Handle errors with try/catch | Use Optional/Result for explicit failure in types |
   207→| Pass dependencies explicitly | Use Environment pattern for implicit config |
   208→

1→---
     2→name: nw-fp-domain-modeling
     3→agent: nw-functional-software-crafter
     4→description: Domain modeling with algebraic data types, smart constructors, and type-level error handling
     5→user-invocable: false
     6→disable-model-invocation: true
     7→---
     8→
     9→# FP Domain Modeling
    10→
    11→Domain modeling with types. Make illegal states unrepresentable, workflows as pipelines, error handling at the type level.
    12→
    13→Cross-references: [fp-principles](./fp-principles.md) | [fp-hexagonal-architecture](./fp-hexagonal-architecture.md) | [fp-algebra-driven-design](./fp-algebra-driven-design.md)
    14→
    15→---
    16→
    17→## 1. The Two Building Blocks
    18→
    19→[STARTER]
    20→
    21→All domain types compose from two operations:
    22→
    23→- **AND (Record Types)**: Value has ALL of these fields. Order requires CustomerInfo AND ShippingAddress AND OrderLines.
    24→- **OR (Choice Types)**: Value is ONE OF these alternatives. ProductCode is either WidgetCode OR GizmoCode.
    25→
    26→Combined recursively, these express virtually any domain structure.
    27→
    28→---
    29→
    30→## 2. Domain Wrappers for Primitives
    31→
    32→[STARTER]
    33→
    34→Never use primitives directly in the domain model. Each domain concept gets its own wrapper type.
    35→
    36→**What**: Wrap primitives so the compiler distinguishes CustomerId from OrderId.
    37→**When**: Every primitive with domain meaning.
    38→**Why**: Prevents accidental mixing (compiler rejects comparing CustomerId with OrderId). Each wrapper carries its own validation rules. The type name IS the documentation.
    39→
    40→---
    41→
    42→## 3. Validated Construction (Smart Constructors)
    43→
    44→[STARTER]
    45→
    46→Raw constructor is private. A `create` function validates input and returns a Result type, making validation failure explicit.
    47→
    48→**Pattern**: UnitQuantity must be between 1 and 1000. Its `create` function rejects values outside that range. A companion `value` function provides read access to the inner primitive.
    49→
    50→**When**: Every domain wrapper with validation rules.
    51→**Why**: Once constructed, a value is guaranteed valid. No defensive checks deeper in the code.
    52→
    53→---
    54→
    55→## 4. Making Illegal States Unrepresentable
    56→
    57→[STARTER]
    58→
    59→The central design guideline. Instead of flags and runtime checks, model the domain so invalid states cannot be constructed.
    60→
    61→### Replace Flags with Distinct Types
    62→
    63→Instead of `{ EmailAddress; IsVerified: bool }`, create separate `VerifiedEmailAddress` and `UnverifiedEmailAddress` types. Functions requiring verification take `VerifiedEmailAddress`, making misuse a compile error.
    64→
    65→### Replace Optional Fields with Choice Types
    66→
    67→Instead of `{ Email: option; Address: option }` (where both could be None), create: `EmailOnly | AddressOnly | EmailAndAddress`. The "at least one required" rule becomes structurally enforced.
    68→
    69→### NonEmptyList for "At Least One" Rules
    70→
    71→Define a type guaranteeing at least one element. Order with `OrderLines: NonEmptyList<OrderLine>` cannot have zero lines.
    72→
    73→---
    74→
    75→## 5. Workflows as Functions
    76→
    77→[STARTER]
    78→
    79→Every business workflow is a single function: Command in, Events out.
    80→
    81→```
    82→PlaceOrderWorkflow : PlaceOrderCommand -> AsyncResult<PlaceOrderEvent list>
    83→```
    84→
    85→### Pipeline Composition
    86→
    87→Workflows decompose into steps, each transforming one document type into the next:
    88→
    89→```
    90→UnvalidatedOrder -> ValidatedOrder -> PricedOrder -> Events
    91→```
    92→
    93→Each step is stateless, pure, has single input/output type, and is independently testable. The workflow assembles by piping steps together.
    94→
    95→**Why**: Pipeline makes the business process visible. Each step name is a domain concept.
    96→
    97→---
    98→
    99→## 6. Document Lifecycle as State Types
   100→
   101→[INTERMEDIATE]
   102→
   103→Rather than one Order type with flags, create separate types for each lifecycle stage:
   104→
   105→- `UnvalidatedOrder` (raw input, all fields strings)
   106→- `ValidatedOrder` (all fields checked)
   107→- `PricedOrder` (prices calculated)
   108→
   109→A top-level Order choice type unifies all states. New states (e.g., `Refunded`) added without breaking existing code.
   110→
   111→**When**: Domain entities with distinct lifecycle stages where different data is available at each stage.
   112→
   113→---
   114→
   115→## 7. State Machines with Types
   116→
   117→[INTERMEDIATE]
   118→
   119→When an entity has distinct states with different data and different allowed operations, model each state as a separate type.
   120→
   121→```
   122→ShoppingCart = EmptyCart | ActiveCart of ActiveCartData | PaidCart of PaidCartData
   123→```
   124→
   125→Transition functions take the choice type, pattern-match on current state, return new state.
   126→
   127→**Benefits**: All states explicit | each state has own data | invalid transitions prevented by types | pattern matching warnings reveal unhandled edge cases.
   128→
   129→---
   130→
   131→## 8. Error-Track Pipelines (Railway Pattern)
   132→
   133→[INTERMEDIATE]
   134→
   135→Each function returns a Result type. Pipeline short-circuits on first failure.
   136→
   137→```
   138→rawInput
   139→  |> validateOrder           -- Result<ValidOrder, Error>
   140→  |> bind calculateTotal     -- Result<PricedOrder, Error>
   141→  |> bind checkInventory     -- Result<ConfirmedOrder, Error>
   142→  |> bind chargePayment      -- Result<PaidOrder, Error>
   143→  |> map generateReceipt     -- Result<Receipt, Error>
   144→```
   145→
   146→**Key combinators**:
   147→- **map**: Transform success value (one-track into two-track)
   148→- **bind**: Chain a function that itself returns Result
   149→- **mapError**: Transform error value
   150→- **tee**: Perform side effect without changing value (logging)
   151→
   152→### Error Classification
   153→
   154→| Category | Examples | Strategy |
   155→|---|---|---|
   156→| Domain Errors | Validation failure, out of stock | Model as types, return via Result |
   157→| Panics | Out of memory, null reference | Throw exceptions, catch at top level |
   158→| Infrastructure Errors | Network timeout, auth failure | Case-by-case |
   159→
   160→### Unifying Error Types
   161→
   162→Each step may have its own error type. Define a common error choice type and use `mapError` to lift step errors before composing.
   163→
   164→### Collecting All Errors (Applicative Validation)
   165→
   166→[ADVANCED]
   167→
   168→Standard bind short-circuits on first error. For validation where you want ALL errors, use Applicative style -- runs all validations and accumulates errors into a list. See [fp-principles](./fp-principles.md) section 5.
   169→
   170→**When**: Form validation | batch input checking | any place user needs all errors at once.
   171→
   172→---
   173→
   174→## 9. Modeling Dependencies
   175→
   176→[INTERMEDIATE]
   177→
   178→Each workflow step declares exactly the functions it needs as parameters:
   179→
   180→```
   181→CheckProductCodeExists : ProductCode -> bool
   182→GetProductPrice        : ProductCode -> Price
   183→```
   184→
   185→**Convention**: Dependencies first in parameter list, primary input last. Enables partial application (functional DI).
   186→
   187→**Public vs. internal**: Top-level workflow hides dependencies from callers. Internal steps make them explicit.
   188→
   189→See [fp-hexagonal-architecture](./fp-hexagonal-architecture.md) section 5 for full DI decision tree.
   190→
   191→---
   192→
   193→## 10. Persistence Ignorance
   194→
   195→[INTERMEDIATE]
   196→
   197→Domain model has no awareness of databases. Two distinct type hierarchies:
   198→
   199→- **Domain types**: Rich, nested, use choice types, validated wrappers. Not serialization-friendly.
   200→- **DTO types**: Flat, primitives, nullable, arrays. Designed for serialization.
   201→
   202→### Conversion Pattern
   203→
   204→- **fromDomain**: Domain -> DTO. Always succeeds.
   205→- **toDomain**: DTO -> Result<Domain, Error>. May fail (validation happens here).
   206→
   207→### Serialization as Pipeline Steps
   208→
   209→```
   210→JSON -> deserialize -> DTO -> toDomain -> [WORKFLOW] -> fromDomain -> DTO -> serialize -> JSON
   211→```
   212→
   213→Domain workflow never sees JSON or DTOs directly.
   214→
   215→---
   216→
   217→## 11. Bounded Contexts
   218→
   219→[ADVANCED]
   220→
   221→Each context has its own dialect of domain language. Contexts communicate only through events and DTOs. Design for autonomy.
   222→
   223→**Trust boundaries**: Input gate validates and converts incoming DTOs to domain types. Output gate converts domain types to DTOs, deliberately dropping private information.
   224→
   225→**Inter-context relationships**: Shared Kernel (shared design) | Customer/Supplier (downstream defines contract) | Anti-Corruption Layer (translator preventing external model from corrupting internal domain).
   226→
   227→---
   228→
   229→## Decision Tree: How to Model This Domain Concept?
   230→
   231→```
   232→Is it a simple value with validation rules?
   233→  YES --> Domain Wrapper + Smart Constructor
   234→  NO -->
   235→    Does it represent one of several alternatives?
   236→      YES --> Choice Type (sum type)
   237→      NO -->
   238→        Does it group several values together?
   239→          YES --> Record Type (product type)
   240→          NO -->
   241→            Does it have distinct lifecycle stages?
   242→              YES --> State Machine with Types (section 7)
   243→              NO -->
   244→                Is it an operation that transforms data through stages?
   245→                  YES --> Workflow Pipeline (section 5)
   246→                  NO --> Evaluate whether it needs modeling at all
   247→```
   248→
   249→---
   250→
   251→## 12. Key Design Heuristics
   252→
   253→1. **Start from events and workflows**, not data structures
   254→2. **Let the domain expert drive naming** -- their vocabulary, not technical jargon
   255→3. **Use types to enforce business rules at compile time** -- every rule in the type system needs no unit test
   256→4. **Document effects in signatures** -- Result for errors | Async for I/O | Option for missing data
   257→5. **Separate domain types from serialization types** -- domain is pure; serialization is infrastructure
   258→6. **Prefer explicit over implicit** -- every input and dependency is a function parameter
   259→
   260→### Naming Patterns
   261→
   262→- **Types as nouns**: Order | ProductCode | CustomerInfo
   263→- **Workflows as verbs**: ValidateOrder | PriceOrder | PlaceOrder
   264→- **Events in past tense**: OrderPlaced | OrderShipped
   265→- **Commands in imperative**: PlaceOrder | ShipOrder | CancelOrder

data.command:
Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

content.thinking:
The issue is that `mcpFetch` with `method: "GET"` is probably passing a body because of how mcpFetch is implemented. Let me check how mcpFetch works — it likely always sends a body with DPoP proof. The fix should be to not pass a body for GET requests, or use a different approach.

content.text: Found it — line 674: `mcpFetch` always sends a body (`JSON.stringify({})` for undefined), which fails for GET requests. The fix is in the acceptance-test-kit.

Name: Inject static headers on MCP client connection
Description: Wire the credential resolver to decrypt static headers and inject them into the MCP client transport when connecting to a server with auth_mode=static_headers. Complete the walking skeleton end-to-end path. The acceptance test uses MSW to mock an MCP server that requires an Authorization header and verifies it receives the correct header.
Acceptance Criteria: When Brain connects to an MCP server configured with static headers, the HTTP request to the MCP server includes the configured Authorization header. tools/list returns tools from the authenticated server.
Test File: tests/acceptance/mcp-server-auth/11-static-headers.test.ts
Scenario Name: "MCP client sends configured headers when connecting to server"
Dependencies: 01-03 (completed)
Files to modify:
  - app/src/server/proxy/credential-resolver.ts (add resolveAuthForMcpServer function)
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/mcp-client.ts (call resolver for auth headers before creating transport)
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/static-headers.ts (add decryptHeaders function if not yet present)

# DESIGN_CONTEXT
Architecture: Modular monolith with pure core / effect shell (functional paradigm).

Credential Resolver Extension (from architecture-design.md):
- resolveAuthForMcpServer(server, deps) → Record<string, string> returns headers map:
  - auth_mode: "none" → empty headers {}
  - auth_mode: "static_headers" → decrypt stored headers, return as plain map
  - auth_mode: "oauth" → load tokens from connected_account, return { Authorization: "Bearer <token>" }
  - auth_mode: "provider" → existing credential_provider flow (unchanged)

Existing credential-resolver.ts already has:
- resolveCredentialsForTool(toolName, identityId, deps) — resolves per-tool credentials
- buildAuthHeaders(credentials) — pure function that builds header map from decrypted credentials
- DecryptedCredentials type and CredentialResult type

The NEW function resolveAuthForMcpServer is a SEPARATE concern — it resolves per-server auth (not per-tool).

MCP Client Factory (mcp-client.ts) already has:
- connect(url, transport, headers?) — headers injected into transport's requestInit
- createTransport(url, transportType, headers?) — passes headers to SSE/StreamableHTTP transport

The connection flow for discovery (server-routes.ts handleDiscover) calls:
  discoverTools({ surreal, mcpClientFactory }, server, { dryRun: true })
Which in discovery.ts calls:
  mcpClientFactory.connect(server.url, server.transport, ???)

The wiring point: discovery.ts needs to resolve auth headers for the server before connecting.

Existing encryption module (encryption.ts):
- encryptSecret(plaintext, keyHex) / decryptSecret(ciphertext, keyHex)
- Key available via deps.config.toolEncryptionKey

static-headers.ts already has:
- encryptHeaders(headers, keyHex) → EncryptedHeaderEntry[]
- It needs decryptHeaders(encrypted, keyHex) → HeaderEntry[] (may already exist from step 01-02)
- buildHeaderMap(headers: HeaderEntry[]) → Record<string, string>

The test scenario (currently skipped with it.skip):
- Creates an MCP server with static_headers via API
- Uses MSW to mock an MCP server that captures requests
- Triggers discovery/connect and asserts the Authorization header is present

IMPORTANT: The test is currently using `it.skip` — you need to remove the `.skip` to enable it.

Important project conventions:
- Do NOT use null. Use undefined via optional properties.
- Use --no-verify -s when committing.
- Functional paradigm.
- NEVER use module-level mutable singletons.
- Pass shared state via dependency injection.

The test body may be a placeholder — implement the full test if needed: create server with static headers via API, set up MSW mock, trigger discovery, assert headers captured.    b. Add resolveAuthForMcpServer to credential-resolver.ts
   c. Wire into discovery.ts or wherever MCP client connections happen — resolve headers before connect()
   d. Run all enabled tests in 11-static-headers.test.ts
4. COMMIT - Stage and commit with conventional message + Step-ID: 01-04 trailer. Use --no-verify -s.

- All tests pass before COMMIT
- No skipped phases without blocked_by reason

# DES_METADATA
Step: 03-05
Feature: mcp-server-auth

# AGENT_IDENTITY
Agent: nw-functional-software-crafter

# SKILL_LOADING
Before starting TDD phases, read your skill files for methodology guidance.
Skills path: ~/.claude/skills/nw-functional-software-crafter/SKILL.md
Always load at PREPARE: tdd-methodology.md, quality-framework.md

# TASK_CONTEXT
Name: Auto-create credential_provider from discovery
Description: On successful OAuth discovery, automatically create a credential_provider record with the discovered authorization_url, token_url, and discovery_source set to the MCP server URL. Update the mcp_server to link to this provider and set auth_mode to "oauth".
Acceptance Criteria: After successful discovery, a credential_provider record exists with discovery_source matching the MCP server URL and authorization/token endpoints matching the discovered values.
Test File: tests/acceptance/mcp-server-auth/12-oauth-discovery.test.ts
Scenario Name: "creates a credential_provider with discovery_source set"
Dependencies: 03-01 (completed)
Files to modify:
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/auth-discovery.ts (add provider creation logic in effect shell or route handler)
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/queries.ts (add createProviderFromDiscovery or reuse existing createProvider)

# DESIGN_CONTEXT
Architecture: Modular monolith with pure core / effect shell (functional paradigm).

On successful discovery, the route handler (handleDiscoverAuth) should:
1. Call discoverAuth to get DiscoveredAuthConfig
2. Create a credential_provider record with:
   - name: derived from auth server hostname (e.g., "auth.example.com")
   - display_name: auth server hostname
   - auth_method: "oauth2"
   - workspace: current workspace
   - authorization_url: discoveredConfig.authorizationEndpoint
   - token_url: discoveredConfig.tokenEndpoint
   - discovery_source: MCP server URL
3. Update mcp_server: set auth_mode = "oauth", provider = new provider record
4. Return provider_id in the discover-auth response

Existing queries.ts has createProvider(surreal, workspaceRecord, content) — reuse it if the shape fits, or extend it.

The test should: call discover-auth, then read the credential_provider directly from SurrealDB and verify discovery_source, authorization_url, token_url.

Project conventions:
- Do NOT use null. Use undefined.
- Use --no-verify -s when committing.
- Functional paradigm.
- The credential_provider table schema includes: name, display_name, auth_method, workspace, authorization_url, token_url, client_id, client_secret_encrypted, scopes, api_key_header, discovery_source, created_at.

# TDD_PHASES
0. PREPARE - Read auth-discovery.ts (handleDiscoverAuth), queries.ts (createProvider), server-routes.ts, test file.
1. RED_ACCEPTANCE - Remove .skip from "creates a credential_provider with discovery_source set". Implement full test. Run — must fail.
2. RED_UNIT - Skip (IO adapter — acceptance test covers the driving port).
3. GREEN - Wire provider creation in handleDiscoverAuth after successful discovery. Update mcp_server with provider link. Run all tests.
4. COMMIT - git commit --no-verify -s with Step-ID: 03-05 trailer.

# QUALITY_GATES
- All tests pass before COMMIT

# OUTCOME_RECORDING
    PYTHONPATH=$HOME/.claude/lib/python python3 -m des.cli.log_phase \
      --project-dir docs/feature/mcp-server-auth/deliver \
      --step-id 03-05 --phase {PHASE_NAME} --status EXECUTED --data PASS
For SKIPPED:
    PYTHONPATH=$HOME/.claude/lib/python python3 -m des.cli.log_phase \
      --project-dir docs/feature/mcp-server-auth/deliver \
      --step-id 03-05 --phase {PHASE_NAME} --status SKIPPED --data "NOT_APPLICABLE: reason"

# RECORDING_INTEGRITY
Valid Skip Prefixes: NOT_APPLICABLE, BLOCKED_BY_DEPENDENCY, APPROVED_SKIP, CHECKPOINT_PENDING
# BOUNDARY_RULES
- Only modify files listed in step's files_to_modify (plus test files and server-routes.ts if handleDiscoverAuth is there)
# TIMEOUT_INSTRUCTION

Name: Generate authorization URL with PKCE S256
Description: Add POST /mcp-servers/:id/authorize endpoint that generates a PKCE code_challenge (S256), state parameter, stores the code_verifier + state temporarily, and returns the full authorization URL with resource parameter (RFC 8707). Extend oauth-flow.ts with PKCE generation and URL building pure functions.
Acceptance Criteria: Response includes redirect_url containing the authorization_endpoint, code_challenge_method=S256, a state parameter, a resource parameter matching the MCP server URI, and a scope parameter.
Test File: tests/acceptance/mcp-server-auth/13-oauth-authorization.test.ts
Scenario Name: "returns authorization URL with code_challenge and state"
Dependencies: 03-05 (completed)
Files to modify:
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/oauth-flow.ts (extend with generatePkce, buildAuthorizationUrl pure functions)
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/server-routes.ts (add handleAuthorize handler)
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/types.ts (add PKCE and authorization types)

# DESIGN_CONTEXT
Architecture: Modular monolith with pure core / effect shell (functional paradigm).

oauth-flow.ts pure core functions (from architecture-design.md):
- generatePkce() → PkceChallenge — S256 code challenge
  - codeVerifier: 43-128 chars, [A-Z]/[a-z]/[0-9]/-._~
  - codeChallenge: BASE64URL(SHA256(codeVerifier))
- buildAuthorizationUrl(params: AuthorizationParams) → string — includes:
  - response_type=code
  - client_id (URL-format for Client ID Metadata Documents, or just brain server base URL for now)
  - redirect_uri (brain's oauth callback URL)
  - code_challenge + code_challenge_method=S256
  - state (CSRF protection)
  - resource parameter (RFC 8707 — MCP server's canonical URI)
  - scope (from discovery)

Types to add:
```typescript
type PkceChallenge = {
  codeVerifier: string;
  codeChallenge: string;
};
type AuthorizationParams = {
  authorizationEndpoint: string;
  clientId: string;
  redirectUri: string;
  codeChallenge: string;
  state: string;
  resource: string;
  scope?: string;
};
```

Route: POST /api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers/:serverId/authorize
Handler should:
1. Load mcp_server, verify it has a linked credential_provider (from discovery)
2. Load the provider to get authorization_endpoint, scopes
3. Generate PKCE pair + random state
4. Store code_verifier + state somewhere temporary (in-memory Map keyed by state, or in SurrealDB)
   For simplicity, use a module-scoped Map<state, { codeVerifier, serverId, workspaceId, createdAt }> with TTL cleanup.
   WAIT — project convention says NO module-level mutable singletons! Use SurrealDB to store the pending auth state instead, or pass through deps.
5. Build authorization URL
6. Return { redirect_url, state }

IMPORTANT: Per AGENTS.md, "Do NOT use module-level mutable singletons". Store the pending OAuth state in SurrealDB (create a simple oauth_pending_state record) or pass it through injected deps. A simple approach: store as a temporary SurrealDB record with TTL.

Actually, simplest approach that respects the convention: store the PKCE state in the mcp_server record itself as pending_oauth fields. Or create an ephemeral table. Let's use a simple approach: store pending_pkce_verifier and pending_oauth_state on the mcp_server record (encrypted). These are cleared after token exchange.

Register route in start-server.ts.

Project conventions:
- Do NOT use null. Use undefined.
- Use --no-verify -s when committing.
- Functional paradigm.
- Do NOT use module-level mutable singletons.

<!-- DES-VALIDATION : required -->
<!-- DES-PROJECT-ID : mcp-server-auth -->

# DES_METADATA
Step: 04-02
Feature: mcp-server-auth
Command: /nw-execute

# AGENT_IDENTITY
Agent: nw-functional-software-crafter

# SKILL_LOADING
Before starting TDD phases, read your skill files for methodology guidance.
Skills path: ~/.claude/skills/nw-functional-software-crafter/SKILL.md
Always load at PREPARE: tdd-methodology.md, quality-framework.md

# TASK_CONTEXT
Name: Exchange authorization code for tokens
Description: OAuth callback receives the authorization code and state, looks up the stored code_verifier from the mcp_server record, and exchanges the code at the token endpoint using PKCE. Add POST /api/workspaces/:wsId/accounts/oauth2/callback endpoint (or GET /oauth/callback depending on existing patterns). The callback looks up which mcp_server has the pending_oauth_state matching the received state param, loads the provider's token_endpoint, and calls exchangeCode.
Acceptance Criteria: Given a valid authorization code and matching state, Brain exchanges the code at the token endpoint with the code_verifier and receives access_token + refresh_token.
Test File: tests/acceptance/mcp-server-auth/13-oauth-authorization.test.ts
Scenario Name: "callback exchanges code using PKCE code_verifier"
Dependencies: 04-01 (completed)
Files to modify:
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/oauth-flow.ts (add exchangeCode effect function + buildTokenRequest pure function)
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/routes.ts (add callback handler, or server-routes.ts)

# DESIGN_CONTEXT
Architecture: Modular monolith with pure core / effect shell (functional paradigm).

oauth-flow.ts functions to add:
Pure core:
- buildTokenRequest(params: TokenExchangeParams) → { url, body, headers }
  - grant_type=authorization_code
  - code, redirect_uri, code_verifier, client_id

Effect shell:
- exchangeCode(config, code, redirectUri, codeVerifier, fetchFn?) → Promise<TokenResult>
  - POSTs to token_endpoint with form-encoded body
  - Returns { access_token, token_type, expires_in?, refresh_token?, scope? }

Types:
```typescript
type TokenExchangeParams = {
  tokenEndpoint: string;
  code: string;
  redirectUri: string;
  codeVerifier: string;
  clientId: string;
};
type TokenResult = {
  access_token: string;
  token_type: string;
  expires_in?: number;
  refresh_token?: string;
  scope?: string;
};
```

Callback flow:
1. Receive code + state from query params (or POST body)
2. Find mcp_server WHERE pending_oauth_state = $state
3. Load the server's linked credential_provider for token_endpoint
4. Call exchangeCode with the code_verifier from the server record
5. Clear pending_pkce_verifier + pending_oauth_state from mcp_server
6. Return tokens (next step 04-03 handles encryption/storage)

For the acceptance test: the test should call authorize first (to get state + stored verifier), then simulate the callback with a mock code. MSW should mock the token endpoint to validate code + code_verifier and return tokens.

Route: POST /api/workspaces/:wsId/accounts/oauth2/callback (check existing routes.ts for the pattern)
Or could be a general GET /oauth/callback endpoint.

Register in start-server.ts.

Project conventions:
- Do NOT use null. Use undefined.
- Use --no-verify -s when committing.
- Functional paradigm.
- Make fetch injectable for testability.

# TDD_PHASES
0. PREPARE - Read oauth-flow.ts, routes.ts, server-routes.ts, 13-oauth-authorization.test.ts, start-server.ts. Check existing OAuth callback patterns.
1. RED_ACCEPTANCE - Remove .skip. Implement full test: discover-auth → authorize → callback with MSW token endpoint mock. Run — must fail.
2. RED_UNIT - Write unit tests for buildTokenRequest pure function.
3. GREEN - Implement exchangeCode, buildTokenRequest, callback handler, register route. Run all tests.
4. COMMIT - git commit --no-verify -s with Step-ID: 04-02 trailer.

# QUALITY_GATES
- All tests pass before COMMIT

# OUTCOME_RECORDING
    PYTHONPATH=$HOME/.claude/lib/python python3 -m des.cli.log_phase \
      --project-dir docs/feature/mcp-server-auth/deliver \
      --step-id 04-02 --phase {PHASE_NAME} --status EXECUTED --data PASS

Add POST /api/workspaces/:wsId/accounts/oauth2/callback endpoint (or GET /oauth/callback depending on existing patterns). The callback looks up which mcp_server has the pending_oauth_state matching the received state param, loads the provider's token_endpoint, and calls exchangeCode.
Acceptance Criteria: Given a valid authorization code and matching state, Brain exchanges the code at the token endpoint with the code_verifier and receives access_token + refresh_token.

# DESIGN_CONTEXT
Architecture: Modular monolith with pure core / effect shell (functional paradigm).

oauth-flow.ts functions to add:
Pure core:
- buildTokenRequest(params: TokenExchangeParams) → { url, body, headers }
  - grant_type=authorization_code
  - code, redirect_uri, code_verifier, client_id

Effect shell:
- exchangeCode(config, code, redirectUri, codeVerifier, fetchFn?) → Promise<TokenResult>
  - POSTs to token_endpoint with form-encoded body
  - Returns { access_token, token_type, expires_in?, refresh_token?, scope? }

Types:
```typescript
type TokenExchangeParams = {
  tokenEndpoint: string;
  code: string;
  redirectUri: string;
  codeVerifier: string;
  clientId: string;
};
type TokenResult = {
  access_token: string;
  token_type: string;
  expires_in?: number;
  refresh_token?: string;
  scope?: string;
};
```

Callback flow:
1. Receive code + state from query params (or POST body)
2. Find mcp_server WHERE pending_oauth_state = $state
3. Load the server's linked credential_provider for token_endpoint
4. Call exchangeCode with the code_verifier from the server record
5. Clear pending_pkce_verifier + pending_oauth_state from mcp_server
6. Return tokens (next step 04-03 handles encryption/storage)

content.text:
All phases complete. Here is the summary:

---

**Step 04-04: Token refresh on expiry -- COMPLETE**

**What was implemented:**

1. **`app/src/server/tool-registry/oauth-flow.ts`** -- Added `refreshAccessToken` effect function. Takes `RefreshTokenParams` (tokenEndpoint, refreshToken, optional clientId/clientSecret) and an injectable `fetchFn`. POSTs `grant_type=refresh_token` to the token endpoint, returns `TokenResult`.

2. **`app/src/server/proxy/credential-resolver.ts`** -- Extended `resolveAuthForMcpServer`:
   - Changed from sync to async (`Promise<Record<string, string>>`)
   - New optional `McpServerAuthDeps` parameter (surreal, toolEncryptionKey, fetchFn)
   - OAuth mode: loads `connected_account` via `oauth_account` reference, checks `token_expires_at` with 60-second buffer, refreshes via `refreshAccessToken` if expired, encrypts and stores new tokens, returns Bearer header
   - On refresh failure: marks account as `expired`, returns `{}`

3. **`app/src/server/tool-registry/discovery.ts`** -- Updated `discoverTools` to `await` the now-async `resolveAuthForMcpServer` and pass `{ surreal, toolEncryptionKey }` deps.

Acceptance Criteria: Given an MCP server with expired access_token but valid refresh_token, the credential resolver refreshes the token, stores the new encrypted token, and returns headers with the new Bearer token.

oauth-flow.ts new function:
- refreshAccessToken(tokenEndpoint, refreshToken, clientId?, fetchFn?) → Promise<TokenResult>
  - POST to token_endpoint with grant_type=refresh_token

credential-resolver.ts resolveAuthForMcpServer extension for auth_mode=oauth:
1. Load connected_account via oauth_account reference
2. Decrypt access_token
3. Check token_expires_at: if expired (or within 60s buffer):
   a. Decrypt refresh_token
   b. Load provider for token_endpoint
   c. Call refreshAccessToken
   d. Encrypt new tokens, update connected_account
4. Return { Authorization: "Bearer <access_token>" }

The resolveAuthForMcpServer function needs access to: surreal (DB), toolEncryptionKey (encryption), and fetch (for refresh calls). These should be passed through deps parameter.

The acceptance test should: seed an mcp_server with oauth mode + connected_account with expired token_expires_at + valid refresh_token. Set up MSW mock for token refresh endpoint. Call discover (triggers resolver). Verify new tokens stored.

content.text:
All phases complete. Here is the summary:

---

**Step 04-04: Token refresh on expiry -- COMPLETE**

**What was implemented:**

1. **`app/src/server/tool-registry/oauth-flow.ts`** -- Added `refreshAccessToken` effect function. Takes `RefreshTokenParams` (tokenEndpoint, refreshToken, optional clientId/clientSecret) and an injectable `fetchFn`. POSTs `grant_type=refresh_token` to the token endpoint, returns `TokenResult`.

2. **`app/src/server/proxy/credential-resolver.ts`** -- Extended `resolveAuthForMcpServer`:
   - Changed from sync to async (`Promise<Record<string, string>>`)
   - New optional `McpServerAuthDeps` parameter (surreal, toolEncryptionKey, fetchFn)
   - OAuth mode: loads `connected_account` via `oauth_account` reference, checks `token_expires_at` with 60-second buffer, refreshes via `refreshAccessToken` if expired, encrypts and stores new tokens, returns Bearer header
   - On refresh failure: marks account as `expired`, returns `{}`

3. **`app/src/server/tool-registry/discovery.ts`** -- Updated `discoverTools` to `await` the now-async `resolveAuthForMcpServer` and pass `{ surreal, toolEncryptionKey }` deps.

4. **`tests/unit/tool-registry/resolve-server-auth.test.ts`** -- Updated 4 existing unit tests to `async`/`await` the new signature. All pass.

5. **`tests/acceptance/mcp-server-auth/13-oauth-authorization.test.ts`** -- Removed `.skip` from scenario 4. Full acceptance test: completes OAuth flow, seeds expired `token_expires_at`, calls `resolveAuthForMcpServer`, asserts refreshed Bearer token returned, verifies new encrypted tokens and future expiry stored in DB.

**Test results:** 4 pass, 2 skip (future scenarios 5-6), 0 fail. Unit tests: 4 pass.
Step 04-04 done. Now **04-05: Refresh failure surfaces auth_error status**.

# TASK_CONTEXT
Name: Refresh failure surfaces auth_error status
Description: When token refresh fails (expired refresh_token or server error), set the MCP server last_status to auth_error so the admin can re-authorize. Add GET /mcp-servers/:id/auth-status endpoint.
Acceptance Criteria: Given an MCP server with both expired access_token and expired refresh_token, after a failed refresh attempt the MCP server last_status is 'auth_error' and auth-status endpoint returns 'expired'.
Test File: tests/acceptance/mcp-server-auth/13-oauth-authorization.test.ts
Scenario Name: "sets last_status to auth_error when refresh fails"
Dependencies: 04-04 (completed)
Files to modify:
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/oauth-flow.ts (error handling in refresh)
  - app/src/server/proxy/credential-resolver.ts (set auth_error on server when refresh fails)
  - app/src/server/tool-registry/server-queries.ts (add updateMcpServerStatus query, add auth-status query)

# DESIGN_CONTEXT
When resolveAuthForMcpServer attempts refresh and it fails:
1. Update mcp_server: set last_status = "auth_error"
2. Mark connected_account status = "expired"
3. Return empty headers (cannot authenticate)

New endpoint: GET /api/workspaces/:wsId/mcp-servers/:id/auth-status
Returns: { auth_status: "connected" | "expired" | "error" | "not_authorized" | "none" }
- "connected": has oauth_account with active status and valid token
- "expired": has oauth_account but token expired and refresh failed
- "error": last_status is auth_error
- "not_authorized": auth_mode is oauth but no oauth_account
- "none": auth_mode is not oauth

Register in start-server.ts.

The test: complete OAuth flow, seed expired tokens, mock token endpoint to reject refresh (400 invalid_grant), trigger resolver, verify last_status = "auth_error" in DB and auth-status endpoint returns "expired".

Project conventions:
- Use --no-verify -s when committing.
- Functional paradigm.

2. RED_UNIT - Skip if IO adapter path.
3. GREEN - Wire auth_error status update on refresh failure. Add auth-status endpoint. Run all tests.
4. COMMIT - git commit --no-verify -s with Step-ID: 04-05 trailer.

- All tests pass before COMMIT
